text: The case for presentation is a laparoscopic segment three liver resection for isolated colorectal metastasis. The patient is a sixty four year old female with stage four colon cancer referred for interval resection of a solitary liver metastasis. Metastatic workup with CT scan and PET imaging confirmed a single metastatic focus in segment three of the liver.

Five trocars are placed for the procedure. The surgeon performs diagnostic laparoscopy to check for disseminated metastatic disease, followed by lysis of previous operative adhesions to facilitate additional port placement. An intraoperative ultrasound is conducted to exclude additional metastatic disease in the liver.

The surgeon scores the surface of the liver with cautery to delineate the boundaries for resection. During this process, an adherent tumor focus is discovered on the distal anterior gastric wall. Due to concerns of metastatic invasion, the affected portion of the stomach is excised en bloc using a harmonic scalpel.

The surgeon elevates the mobilized left lobe of the liver and scores the posterior boundaries of the liver resection using cautery. Following the marking of resection boundaries, cautery energy is increased to begin deeper division of the hepatic parenchyma.

The surgeon approaches the liver from the left lateral side due to midline postoperative adhesions and port angulation. The thin lateral substance of the liver allows for parenchymal transection using an endo-GIA stapler with 3.5 mm staples.

The surgeon applies gentle opposing traction and cautery to dissect deeper into the hepatic parenchyma. As the dissection progresses, the harmonic scalpel is used to control the division of liver tissue and seal larger blood vessels within the segment.

Small peripheral vessels within the hepatic parenchyma are dissected free and clipped proximally and distally using a laparoscopic clipapplier. The vessels are then divided, and the surgeon continues parenchymal transection using a technique similar to the open finger fracture method.

Vascular clips are applied to hepatic vessels to control minor bleeding, allowing further parenchymal transection. As the dissection progresses deeper, larger segmental vessels are isolated and divided using an endo-GIA stapler with 2.5 mm staples. The harmonic scalpel is used to continue dissection at the lateral edge of the falciform ligament until another large vessel is encountered. The surrounding parenchyma is thinned to facilitate the application of the endoscopic stapler, and the division of this vessel completely separates the specimen from the liver.

The surgeon uses high-energy cautery to coagulate the remaining raw surface of the liver, achieving hemostasis with minimal oozing. Surgicel is applied in specific areas alongside cautery to further control bleeding.

The surgeon closes the remaining gastric defect transversely using a two-layer closure technique. A running 2-0 Vicryl suture is applied to the inner layer, followed by interrupted 2-0 silk sutures on the outer layer to prevent narrowing of the distal stomach.
The resected liver specimen is placed into a large endobag and removed through an enlarged port site incision.
